coredata.dc:title: Magnetofluorescent Fe3O4/carbon quantum dots coated single-walled carbon nanotubes as dual-modal targeted imaging and chemo/photodynamic/photothermal triple-modal therapeutic agents
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ming
Wang, Wentao
Cui, Yingjun
Chu, Xiaohong
Sun, Baohong
Zhou, Ninglin
Shen, Jian
coredata.dc:description: In this work, PEG 2000N modified Fe3O4@carbon quantum dots (CQDs) coated single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs) are fabricated and utilized as a multifunctional platform for imaging-guided collaborative treatment of cancer when the obtained agents act as dual photodynamic and photothermal effect under 808 nm laser irradiation. A model chemotherapy drug, doxorubicin (DOX), could be loaded into the pore structure of the obtained SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs nano-carriers with high efficiency. These magnetofluorescent SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs were conjugated with a sgc8c aptamer, denoted as SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs-DOX-Apt, for targeting dual modalfluorescence/magnetic resonance (MR) imaging. Multifunctional SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs-DOX-Apt had a strong effect on the targeted lung cancer cells, in the manner of inducing photodynamic and photothermal ablation, and was found to release DOX rapid following irradiation with pH/NIR laser. The current research demonstrates that SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs-DOX-Apt nanocomposites can be used as an efficient nanoplatform for combing cancer photothermal therapy (PTT), photodynamic therapy (PDT) and chemotherapy (CT).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanotubes
Photodynamic
Photothermal
sgc8c aptamer
Chemotherapy

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Drug loading and drug release studies
In vivo MR imaging of tumor
In vivo photothermal imaging and CT/PDT/PTT

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic procedure of SWCNTs-PEG (A), GP-Fe3O4@CQDs (B) and SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs (C).

(A) TEM (a) and HRTEM (b, c) images of CQDs. (B) TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images of Fe3O4@CQDs. (C) TEM images of SWCNTs-PEG. (D) TEM images of GP-Fe3O4@CQDs, and EDS element mapping of Fe, C and N elements throughout the GP-Fe3O4@CQDs. (E) TEM (a, b) and HRTEM (c, d) images of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs.

(A) XRD patterns of pure Fe3O4, Fe3O4@CQDs, and SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs. (B) XPS spectrum of pure Fe3O4@CQDs and SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs. (C) PL spectra of Fe3O4@CQDs (a, λex = 340 nm), SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs (b, λex = 340; c, λex = 350 nm), free DOX (d, λex = 500 nm) and SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX (e, λex = 350; f, λex = 500 nm). (D) UV–vis-NIR absorbance spectra of Fe3O4@CQDs, SWCNTs, PEG, SWCNTs-PEG, and SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs.

(A) Magnetic hysteresis curves of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs. (B) Photo-degradation of MB in the presence of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs under NIR light irradiation (λ = 800 nm). (C) The possible catalytic mechanism for SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs under visible light. (D) The EPR spectra of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs in solution. (E) Laser power dose-dependent temperature elevation of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs solution (50 µg/mL) under 808 nm laser irradiation for 5 min (initial temperature 22 °C). (F) The T2 relaxation time of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs from M.R.I. and digital images of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs as contrast agents, as a function of increasing Fe concentration.

(A) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and corresponding pore-size distribution curve (inset) of the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs. (B) UV–vis-NIR absorption spectra of DOX, SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs and SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX. (C) The plot of DOX loading amount for SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs versus the drug concentration. (D) Drug release profile of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX at different conditions within 24 h. Dot lines refer to NIR irradiation process. (E) Fluorescence images of calcein AM/PI-stained HeLa cells incubated with SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt (a) and SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX (b). The scale bar: 100 μm. (F) Relative viability of HeLa cells incubated with SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt and SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX.

(A) Fluorescence images of calcein AM/PI-stained HeLa cancer cells incubated with various media: (a) PBS + laser (2 W/cm2), (b) SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs-Apt only (50 µg/mL), (c) 50 µg/mL of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt (CT), (d) 50 µg/mL of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs-Apt + laser (808 nm, 0.5 W/cm2, PDT), (e) 50 µg/mL of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt + laser (808 nm, 0.5 W/cm2, CT/PDT) and (f) 50 µg/mL of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt + laser (808 nm, 2 W/cm2, CT/PDT/PTT). Scale bar = 100 µm. Relative viability of HBE cells (B) and HeLa cells (C) through different treatments: CT (0–60 µg/mL), PDT (0–60 µg/mL), CT/PDT (0–60 µg/mL) and CT/PDT/PTT (0–60 µg/mL). (D) In vivo T2 weight MRI images and color-mapped images of mice bearing tumor after intravenous injection of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs at different timed intervals.

(A) IR thermal images of HeLa tumor-bearing mice incubated with various media: (a) PBS, (b) 100 µg/mL of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs-Apt + laser (808 nm, 0.5 W/cm2), (c) 100 µg/mL of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt + laser (808 nm, 0.5 W/cm2) and (d) 100 µg/mL of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt + laser (808 nm, 2 W/cm2). (B) Heating curve of the five laser-irradiated groups. (C) Representative photographs of tumor-bearing mice after different treatments. (D) Time-dependent tumor growth curves observed after different treatments (n = 5, P < .05 for each group). (E) H&E staining of tumor sections gathered from various treatment groups of mice on day 2. Scale bar = 50 µm.

(A) H&E staining of various organs sections gathered (14 days) from various treatment groups of mice on day 2. Scale bar = 50 µm. (B) Comparison of the organ indices of various treatment groups after 14 days of inoculation. (C) Optical images of RBCs treated with after injection of DOX, SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs and SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt. (D) Serum biochemistry data for mice injected with SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs after 10 h and 10 days post-injection.

head.ce:title: Magnetofluorescent Fe3O4/carbon quantum dots coated single-walled carbon nanotubes as dual-modal targeted imaging and chemo/photodynamic/photothermal triple-modal therapeutic agents

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials
Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Bio-functional Materials
School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, PEG 2000N modified Fe3O4@carbon quantum dots (CQDs) coated single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs) are fabricated and utilized as a multifunctional platform for imaging-guided collaborative treatment of cancer when the obtained agents act as dual photodynamic and photothermal effect under 808 nm laser irradiation. A model chemotherapy drug, doxorubicin (DOX), could be loaded into the pore structure of the obtained SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs nano-carriers with high efficiency. These magnetofluorescent SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs were conjugated with a sgc8c aptamer, denoted as SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs-DOX-Apt, for targeting dual modalfluorescence/magnetic resonance (MR) imaging. Multifunctional SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs-DOX-Apt had a strong effect on the targeted lung cancer cells, in the manner of inducing photodynamic and photothermal ablation, and was found to release DOX rapid following irradiation with pH/NIR laser. The current research demonstrates that SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs-DOX-Apt nanocomposites can be used as an efficient nanoplatform for combing cancer photothermal therapy (PTT), photodynamic therapy (PDT) and chemotherapy (CT).

ce:section.ce:para: Taken the recent interest in photodynamic therapy (PDT) owing to its noninvasiveness and fewer side effects, medical researchers undertake an investigation of its role in cancer treatment [1,2]. In PDT, a photosensitizer absorbs a specific wavelength, and the absorbed light energy is transferred to an electron, which attacks oxygen to oxidize toxic free radicals, also known as reactive oxygen species (ROS) [3,4]. Given its high degree of chemical reactivity, ROS disrupts organelles, such as mitochondrial membranes, upon entry into the cells. On the basis of these reported characteristics, PDT can be proposed to be a better option for cancer therapy because it does not have the drawbacks of traditional therapy [5].

The implementation of photothermal therapy (PTT) relies on the development of photothermal coupling agents [6]. Carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene, single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs), and single-wall carbon nanohorns (SWCNTs), have become increasingly popular for PTT [7]. Pioneering studies have shown that SWCNTs can be used to deliver therapeutic drugs and diagnostic molecules into cells [8]. SWCNTs can also absorb light in the near-infrared region and cause cell death by localized photothermal effects. The use of SWCNTs would help develop innovative multimodal therapies that combine PTT, PDT, and chemotherapy (CT). In a sense, PDT as a noninvasive phototherapy currently used in clinical practice, providing the rationality and scientificalness about the combination of PTT with PDT and CT with clinical value [9].

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have emerged as promising candidates for highly efficient delivery of drugs and biomolecules as their unique structure and properties [10]. CNTs can be united with anti-cancer drugs either by covalent or noncovalent bonding. Some cancer chemotherapy agents with extended π-structures larger than one aromatic ring, such as DOX, can be easily bound to CNTs non-covalently by strong pep interactions [11]. It would be much more desirable if CNTs-anticancer drug conjugates were equipped with tumor-targeting ligands or antibodies. Tumor-targeting formulations in such a way can not only improve their internalization efficiency but also minimize the potential toxicity [12].

Triggered drug release by an external stimulus such as NIR irradiation could significantly advance cancer treatment by overcoming the indiscriminate drug distribution observed in conventional drug delivery [13,14]. The combination of PTT, PDT, and CT, termed chemo-phototherapy, can achieve enhanced anti-cancer efficacy via synergistic effects [15]. The main purpose of this work is to present a new strategy in biomedical nanotechnology that allows simultaneous PTT, PDT and CT [16,17].

Herein, we report a novel, multifunctional therapeutic agent-doxorubicin-loaded, sgc8c aptamer-conjugated and SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs which can selectively target cancer cells and kill them via photodynamic/photothermal-induced drug release. SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs have a strong ability to transform 808-nm NIR light into heat energy, ROS, and ablate cancer cells. We produced SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs-DOX-Apt with excellent water dispersibility, high stability (in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) buffer and NaCl solutions), and excellent magnetic properties which provide a plausible potential for fluorescence/MR imaging and drug delivery [18]. The stimulation of pH/NIR-light to the release of DOX does not occur randomly but rather in a targeted event, enabling intracellular drug accumulation and further enhancing the efficiency to kill cancer cells. In vivo combination therapy is finally carried out with SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs-DOX-Apt, demonstrating outstanding tumor-suppression effect by single treatment using PDT, CT or CT/PDT.

Raw single-walled carbon nanotubes were purchased from Golden Innovation Business Co., Ltd (Taipei, Taiwan). Calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) were supplied from AnaSpec (Fremont, CA). PEG 2000N polymers were purchased from PegBio, Suzhou, China. Culture medium and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from SunShine Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Dialysis membranes of 500–1000 Da (USA, Spectrumlabs) were purchased from Toscience Biotechnology Co, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). sgc8c aptamer (5′-NH2-TGA ATG TTG TTT TTT CTC TTT TCT ATA GTA-3′) was purchased from Sangon Biological Engineering Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2010F, 200 kV) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS, Thermo Fisher Scientific). The XRD patterns were recorded using a Rigaku/Max-3A X-ray diffractometer. The hysteresis loops were obtained with a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM 7407, LakeShore). UV–Vis-NIR spectroscopy measurements were performed on a Cary 5000 UV–vis-NIR spectrometer (Varian). The ζ potential measurement and DLS analysis were performed on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 Analyzer (Malvern). The FT-IR spectrum was recorded on a Nicolet 5700 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. The magnetic hysteresis loop was created using a Quantum Design MPMS-XL 5 superconducting quantum interference device. A Siemens Trio TIM 3 Tesla MRI was used to measure the T2 relaxation times of sample. The fluorescent images of cells were acquired by laser scanning confocal microscope (TI-E-A1R, Nikon, Japan). Details of methods used for material characterization are described in the “Section 2” (Supporting Information).

For the preparation of the composites consisting of CNTs and QDs, the SWCNTs surfaces need to be functionalized in order to provide the required amount of oxygen containing groups (OCGs) on the tubes as well as to shorten the length of the as-received tubes. Therefore, the SWCNTs were initially oxidized and shortened to introduce carboxylic acid groups onto the side walls of SWCNTs [19]. We interpret the typical TEM (Fig. S1B) as a clear indication of the original SWCNTs have been cut short after the oxidation. Subsequently, the branched PEG 2000N was covalently grafted on the shortened SWCNTs via the formation of amides between the carboxylic acid of SWCNTs-COOH and the amine of PEG 2000N [12].

The morphology and nanostructures of the resulting SWCNTs-PEG nanohybrids were characterized by TEM. The outer wall of the SWCNTs was coated with a polymer shell along the stretched direction of the tube and there were obvious differences between the SWCNTs sheets and the polymer shell [8]. The thickness of polymer shell was 5–7 nm, as marked in Fig. 1 C. Such core-shell nanostructures were the same as those of polymer-grafted SMWNTs reported before [19]. The observations of TEM confirmed the covalent grafting of PEG 2000N onto SWCNTs. Fig. S2 shows the FT-IR spectra of SWCNTs-COOH and the as-prepared SWCNTs-PEG. The SWCNTs-COOH presents an absorption band at 1709 cm−1, which is accounts for the carbonyl stretch of the carboxylic acid group. After grafting of PEG 2000N, new absorption bands appear at 1651 and 1482 cm−1, which are corresponding to the amide carbonyl vibration and the NH bending vibration, respectively. The intensity of the absorption band at 1709 cm−1 attributed to CO of OOH was significantly reduced, indicating the consumption of most COOH groups in the carboxy-terminated CNTs due to the formation of amide structure after the graft of PEG 2000N. The N 1s peak of the SWCNTs-PEG (Fig. S3A) centered at 399.3 eV was associated with aromatic N and nitrogen in an NH2 state on the surface [20]. The initial SWCNTs-COOH have a zeta potential of −22.3 mV, which changes to −10.2 mV when modified with PEG 2000N (Fig. S3B). The difference in the weight loss at 430 °C between SWCNTs-PEG and SWCNTs-COOH showed that the PEG 2000N grafting amount is about 17.8% (Fig. S4A).

The CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method which is fully depicted in Fig. S4B. In brief, citric acid (CA) and PGA was dissolved in DI water, and then heated to produce CQDs [21]. The morphology and structures of the resultant CQDs are further elucidated by TEM and HRTEM images. Fig. 2 A and Fig. S5A displays the TEM and HRTEM image of the as-prepared CQDs, indicating that they are spherical particles with a mean particle diameter of 2.8 ± 0.3 nm determined from 150 counts of the particles. DLS measurements revealed that the prepared CQDs exhibited with a narrow size distribution between 4.0 and 8.0 nm (Fig. S5B). This value is also consistent with the size determined from TEM images because DLS measures the overall hydrodynamic size of particles. Fig. 2A, b shows the HRTEM image of CQDs with the lattice spacing around 0.21 nm, which agrees well with the crystallographic (0 0 2) spacing of carbon [22]. Fe3O4@CQDs was fabricated by simultaneously forming superparamagnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles and CQDs. This approach, schematically illustrated in Fig. 1, started with the dispersion of FeCl3·6H2O and CMC in DI water. In this process, Fe3+ was captured by hydroxyl, carboxyl or amino groups on the CMC by coordination. Subsequently, the solid (Fe-CMC) was dispersed by refluxing ethanol with a round bottom flask [23]. Fe-CMC (0.5 g) was dissolved in a mixture of CQDs and NaOAc solution, and then the mixture was sonicated until a homogeneous solution formed. Then the resulting homogeneous solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and sealed before heating at 200 °C for 10 h. Fig. 2B, b displays a HRTEM image of the sectional hybrid nanoparticles showing a single CQDs and Fe3O4 nanocrystal. Under HRTEM, a region of magnetite (Fe3O4) within a CQDs can be identified by the (2 2 0) lattice spacing of 0.345 nm. Similarly, an inter-planar distance in the area of the lattice fringe is 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (0 0 2) lattice planes of graphitic (sp2) carbon [24]. These results demonstrate that the CQDs are successfully loaded on the surface of Fe3O4 particles.

GP-Fe3O4@CQDs were prepared by a one-pot cross-linking reaction of Fe3O4@CQDs and GP. Fig. 2D shows the TEM image of the as-prepared GP-Fe3O4@CQDs. After Fe3O4@CQDs are introduced into the GP molecules, spherical Fe3O4@CQDs with an average diameter of 5.6 nm are evenly distributed within the GP matrix, resulting from the strong anchoring effect between GP and Fe3O4@CQDs. The EDS element mapping result (elements distribution of Fe, C and N) further confirms that Fe3O4@CQDs are evenly distributed within the GP matrix.

Fe3O4@CQDs was conjugated to the surface of PEG-SWCNTs, using a traditional EDC/Sulfo-NHS chemical reaction, in order to endow Fe3O4@CQDs targeting with dual MR/fluorescence imaging. Interestingly, the morphologies of SWCNTs decorated by PEG with helical conformation are shown in Fig. 2E, F. One can clearly observe that helical structures are indeed formed for all the SWCNTs bundles in SWCNTs systems. Thus, it can be concluded that the formation of the helical structure will become easy by using SWCNTs bundles with “groove structure” [25-28]. This could also be due to the peculiar “groove structure” on the surface of SWCNTs bundles, which could facilitate the orientation of these polymer chains and the lateral formation of the stable nucleus, thus making it much easier to form the helical structure [29]. Simultaneously, the anionic Fe3O4 nanoparticles can be also scattered in cationic SWCNTs-PEG by an electrostatic self-assembly approach. This suggests that the CQDs are strongly attached to the SWCNTs-PEG as they are not detached even when exposed to the high sonication energy [19]. The Fe3O4 nanoparticles could be clearly observed in the helical structures of SWCNTs-PEG. This suggests that a CNTs-reinforced magnetic helical structures can be easily fabricated from the Fe3O4-loaded SWCNTs-PEG, which is promising for potential applications of the resulting magnetic nanocomposites. A typical TEM image of SWCNTs-PEG was shown in Fig. 2E, where the Fe3O4@CQDs exhibited a round-like geometry with a diameter of 6.0 nm, and highly dispersible in water. Fig. S6 shows the EDS analysis of the corresponding specimen of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4. The signals of C, Fe, O and N elements demonstrated that the Fe3O4 particles had been scattered in the SWCNTs-PEG. Helical structure with polymer periodically patterning on SWCNTs bundles has potential in quite a few application fields and offers oncotherapy, catalysis and morphology interest [30–33]. Understanding of its formation mechanism is important. The formation mechanism [34,35] is described in the Fig. S7 (Supporting Information).

To obtain the structural information about SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs, X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization was conducted. Fe3O4@CQDs is crystalline (Fig. 3 A), with a (2 1 0) peak at 2θ = 23.8°, a (2 1 3) peak at 2θ = 28.6°, a (2 2 0) peak at 2θ = 30.7°, a strong (3 1 1) peak at 2θ = 35.4°, a (4 0 0) peak at 2θ = 43.1°, a (4 2 2) peak at 2θ = 57.0° and a (5 1 1) peak at 2θ = 62.7°. This corresponds with the JCPDS 19-0629 card for magnetite, as Fe3O4 possesses the (2 1 3), (3 1 1), (2 2 0), (5 1 1) and (4 2 2) peaks that are common to both magnetite and maghemite but lacks of the characteristic (2 1 0) peaks of maghemite (JCPDS 39-1346) [24]. Seven diffraction lines were observed in the representative XRD pattern of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs at 2θ = 21.4°, 25.1°, 30.1°, 35.6°, 43.2°, 53.6°, 57.1° and 62.5° that can be assigned to the (0 0 2), (2 1 0), (2 2 0), (3 1 1), (4 0 0), (4 2 2), (5 1 1) and (4 4 0) reflections, respectively [36]. The SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs curve shows a change in structure from both SWCNTs and Fe3O4@CQDs. SWCNTs can be identified by the weak (2 1 0) peak at 2θ = 25.1°. The intensity of peak (4 0 0) has shifted by 0.6° when compared to the SWCNTs curve, which may stem from the reaction of SWCNTs and Fe3O4@CQDs. The effect of SWCNTs within the Fe3O4@CQDs has caused a reduction in intensity of the (4 2 2) and (0 0 2) peaks from Fe3O4@CQDs, and the introduction of a (2 1 0) peak at 2θ = 25.1°.

Detailed information about the composition of the samples was provided by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements. Fig. 3B displays the survey X-ray photoelectron spectral (XPS) scan of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs, revealing that SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs are mainly composed of carbon, phosphorus, nitrogen, and oxygen with the binding energy peaks at 528.78, 400.52, 283.34 and 708.83 eV corresponding to O 1s, N 1s, C 1s and Fe 2p, respectively. The results in the inset of Fig. 3B revealed the presence of C 67.33, N 11.29, O 20.08 and Fe (calculated) 1.3 wt%. Which further confirmed that the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs nanocomposites were synthesized successfully. In the Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. S8), the peaks for Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 were observed at 708.34 and 724.13 eV, which is also indicative of the formation of a Fe3O4 phase in the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs matrix [36].

To obtain the further insight about the interaction between Fe3O4@CQDs and SWCNTs, we examined the PL spectra of Fe3O4@CQDs and SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs (Fig. 3C). It is indicated that the PL of Fe3O4@CQDs is obviously quenched in the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs system. As previously demonstrated, the quenching suggests an energy transfer from Fe3O4@CQDs into SWCNTs in three possible ways: hole transfer, electron transfer and both [37]. At present, we believe the dominant mechanism is most likely of electron transfer from the conduction band of the Fe3O4@CQDs to the empty electronic states of the SWCNTs, since SWCNTs are known to have a high electron affinity, as also seen in many previous studies. When the Fe3O4@CQDs are brought together with CNTs intensity of the excitonic emission of Fe3O4@CQDs decreases, a process called fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, the emission wavelength of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs (Fig. S9A) shifts from 330 to 390 nm when the excitation wavelength increasing gradually from 420 to 480 nm. This behavior is attributed to the surface states which affect the band gap of Fe3O4@CQDs. The surface state is similar to a molecular state, whereas the size impact is a result of quantum dimensions, both of which are attributed to the complexity of the excited states of Fe3O4@CQDs [25].

UV–vis absorption was employed to confirm the PEG 2000N molecules were conjugated on the surface of the SWCNTs. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of SWCNTs, PEG 2000N, and SWCNTs-PEG are shown in Fig. 3D. The free PEG 2000N molecules exhibited characteristic peaks at 237 nm. The same PEG peaks were observed for PEG conjugated SWCNTs, with a blue shift from 237 nm to 245 nm; thus, indicating successful conjugation of the PEG 2000N ligand to SWCNTs, because of the formation of an amide bond linkage between PEG 2000N and SWCNTs. Fig. 3D shows the measured spectra on the supernatant of the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs and compares them to a spectrum measured for the Fe3O4@CQDs solution (as-synthesized Fe3O4@CQDs before the attachment). Curiously, the same Fe3O4@CQDs peaks were observed for Fe3O4@CQDs conjugated SWCNTs-PEG, with a blue shift from 284 nm to 280 nm, which suggests that Fe3O4@CQDs used in the synthesis were attached to the SWCNTs-PEG.

Hysteresis loops for the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs measured at 300 K exhibited ferromagnetic behavior as shown in Fig. 4 A. Magnetization loops show that the saturation magnetization value of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs is 13.2 emu/g at 300 K. As demonstrated in the inset of Fig. 4A, the magnetic moment of the Fe3O4 nanocrystals on the surface is sufficiently high; therefore, the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs probe can be easily collected by an external magnet, and this further reveals the strong magnetism of the as-prepared nanocomposites, which makes magnetic separation possible.

The SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs we prepared have excellent up-converted PL, and they can be excited by long-wavelength light (λ > 600 nm) with the up-converted emissions located in the range of 420–520 nm. Fig. S9B shows the up-converted PL spectra of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs with excitations at 600, 700, 800, 900 and 1000 nm. The upconversion PL property of the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs can be attributed to the multiphoton active process similar to the previously reported carbon dots. This result suggests that the up-converted PL response of Fe3O4@CQDs can indeed responsible for the biological, photoelectrical and photocatalytic applications of the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs composite in the NIR range. The completely disappearance of the color of the bright blue MB solution which had been added 100 μg/mL SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs and treated for 20 min with NIR irradiation beforehand in the insets of Fig. 4B, revealing that the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs are highly efficient catalysts for the photodegradation of MB. When the samples were illuminated with NIR light, the light with a shorter wavelength (from 420 to 520 nm) will be emitted as a result of the upconversion PL property of the CQDs. These photo-induced light at the shorter wavelengths can, in turn, excite the Fe3O4 nanocrystals to form electron-hole (e−-h+) pairs (Fig. 4C). These photon-excited electrons can freely transfer in the conducting network of CQDs because of their electron-storage capacity. Moreover, the CQDs serving as an electron reservoir can suppress the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The produced electron-hole pairs reacted with the adsorbed oxidants/reducers (usually O2/OH−) on the surface of the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs to produce ROS (e.g.
O2 −,
OH), which subsequently cause the degradation of MB dye molecules [37]. This mechanism is supported by the experimental evidence of the hydroxyl radicals (OH) produced by the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs under NIR-light irradiation (Fig. S10A). This mechanism is further supported by the experimental results that the presence of N2 purging, hole scavenger EDTA (1 mM), and a hydroxyl radical scavenger t-BuOH (1 mM) can significantly reduce the photo-degradation fraction of MB under the same conditions using the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs as a catalyst (Fig. S10B). Therefore, it was found that the resulting SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs produce ROS for PDT providing the promising biomedical application [15].

The ability of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs solutions to produce ROS with and without photo-excitation was examined. We used 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as 1O2 and OH
(or
O2 −) trappers, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4D, the 808 nm photo-excitation of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline n-oxide (DMPO) solution results in the formation of DMPO-OH spin adducts. No other ROS (1O2) signals were detected (Fig. 4D, bottom). These results confirm that the electron transferring, instead of energy from the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs to oxygen is the predominant process during the sensitization of ground-state oxygen [1], which is also consistent with photo-degradation mechanism for SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs under NIR light irradiation.

The ROS sensor, 2,7-dichlorofluorescein (DCF), was used to measure the in vitro ROS generation ability of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs. As shown in Fig. S11A, the remarkable fluorescence of DCF was detected in the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs with 808 nm laser group, verifying the unaffected ROS generation ability under light irradiation. It is well known that the high levels of ROS can increase cellular oxidative stress and trigger cell death via autophagy, apoptosis or necrosis [3]. Exposure of nanomaterials may cause generation of ROS inside cells. To examine the induction of oxidative stress by SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs, we monitor the cellular ROS generation by flow cytometry using a nonfluorescent 2,7-dichlorofuorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), which can be oxidized by intracellular ROS to become highly fluorescent 2,7-dichlorofuorescein (DCF). HeLa cells were treated with various concentrations of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs for 3 h under reduced serum conditions. Fig. S11B clearly indicates that the ROS levels induced by SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs in HeLa cells are slightly higher than the control group. These promising results have led us to further develop SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs polypyridyl complexes as PDT agents.

The NIR effect of the probes was studied to identify the proper laser power and irradiation time in vivo. An IR thermal camera was used to monitor the temperature in vivo (inset of Fig. 4E). DI water was used as the negative control. Over the years, a number of researches have been provided to explain the temperature of ablating cancer cells what exceeds 42.5 °C. Fig. 4E shows that, with increasing laser power, the temperature can reach approximately 35.5 °C, even up to 50.8 °C under 2 W/cm2. With increasing laser time, the temperature of the solution increased noticeably. Fig. 4E shows that, compared with the experimental group, the temperature of the DI water group shows no obvious changes with different laser powers, suggesting that the potential of these nanoparticles in PTT and the enhancing effects of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs should be explored further in cells [38]. To investigate the photothermal conversion efficiency of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs further, we recorded the temperature change of the solution (50 µg/mL, 1.0 mL) function as time under continuous irradiation of 808 nm laser (2 W/cm2) until the solution reached a steady-state temperature (Fig. S12A). According to the obtained data (Fig. S12B), the photothermal conversion efficiency can be calculated to be about 38.9%.

In order to evaluate the T2 contrast properties of the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs, T2-weighted images of the as-prepared SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs dispersed in DI water with different concentrations (0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9 and 1.1 mM) were obtained in a Siemens Trio TIM 3 Tesla M.R.I. Clear, positive, and increasing contrast enhancement of MR signals occurred concurrently with a gradual increase in SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs concentration. The T2 relaxation time of the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs from MRI with the slope of the relaxation time determined as 16.419 mM−1s−1 (Fig. 4F). The relaxation time slopes of nanoparticles that contain Fe3O4 vary is of the different Fe concentrations of among the used-nanoparticles. These results indicate that the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs have high potential as T2 contrast agents for MR imaging diagnosis [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading and drug release studies
ce:section.ce:para: The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm of the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs contained pore interiors and its corresponding pore size distribution curve is shown in Fig. 5 A. The isotherm can be classified as type IV isotherms characteristic of mesoporous materials. The inset of Fig. 5A shows an ink-bottle-type IV N2 adsorption isotherm, implying the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs possess a pore structure. The corresponding pore size distribution data calculated from the branch of the nitrogen isotherm desorption by the BJH (Barrett-Joyner-Halenda) method shows a narrow pore size distribution peaked at 1.7 nm. The specific surface area can reach 181.5 m3/g calculated from the linear part of the BET (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller) plot. The single point adsorption total volume at P/P0 = 0.989 is 1.10 cm3/g. The pore structure and luminescent properties of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs make them suitable for use as drug carriers [13].

A clear understanding of drug release behavior of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX is beneficial to evaluate the efficacy of chemotherapy in cancer ablation. PBS solutions at pH 7.5 and 5.5 are applied to mimic micro-environments of normal body and tumor, respectively [10]. An 808-nm laser (2 W/cm2) is used to mimic PTT. As shown in Fig. 5D, for SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX in PBS solution, the accumulative drug release rate achieves 10.6% in which DOX escapes from SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX spontaneously via Brownian movement while the accumulative drug release rate reaches 20.6% in PBS solution (pH 5.5). In this case, DOX escapes from SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX and dissolves quickly via protonation effect in an acidic environment, leading to a higher release rate at the same time point. Once the 808-nm laser is applied during this drug release process, a fast drug release is observed and the release rate reaches 61.4%. During this process, sharp increases occur at time points of 0.5, 10 and 20 h (Fig. 5D), with the release rate being 12.8%, 9.2%, and 7.6%, respectively. The irradiation of 808-nm laser causes a temperature to rise, which speeds up the molecular movement of DOX and thus greatly accelerates its escape from SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX. Our results demonstrate that SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX possess both pH and heat responsive drug release behavior in which heat plays a dominant role. The above results provide solid evidence for the feasibility of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX in chemotherapy.

The aptamer targeting domains the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX to specifically target cancer cells. The aptamer was conjugated to the carboxyl group of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX coupling method. The conjugation of the Apt to SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX was confirmed by calcein AM/PI double stained images of cells [40]. The cells were stained with both propidium iodide (PI) and calcein AM to identify dead and live cells, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5E within the region of the laser spot, SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt caused severe chemical, photothermal and photodynamic injury to cancer cells and almost all of the cells were dead, as indicated by the intense homogeneous red fluorescence. In contrast, all the cells displayed green fluorescence in the region when HeLa cells were incubated with SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX. Subsequently, an MTT assay was performed to further evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt and SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX on cancer cells. As shown in Fig. 5F, after 0.5 h of incubation with SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX (equivalent concentration of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt) upon 1.0 W/cm2 of laser irradiation, the viabilities of HeLa cells decreased significantly, and less than 50% of HeLa cells remained alive. As a counterpart of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX, SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt exhibited much higher cytotoxicity against HeLa cells, indicating that SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt can potentially trigger much more severe chemical, photothermal and photodynamic injury to cancer cells via the enhanced cellular uptake. In our SWCNTs-PEG system, DOX is loaded inside the Fe3O4@CQDs and the polymer surface. Next, we wondered whether drug release behavior of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt triggered by the NIR light would also exist inside cells. Details of the result are described in the “Fig. S14” (Supporting Information).

Hemolysis rate is an important factor for the characterization of blood compatibility. Fig. S15A showed the hemolytic activities of free DOX SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs and SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt. SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt did not show detectable hemolytic activities on red blood cells (RBC) in the experimental DOX concentration (100 μg/mL). It can be seen that the hemolysis rate of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt was significantly lower than that of DOX. This results showed that the prepared drug-delivery system had better safety and could avoid the bleeding caused by carrier during injection; thus, the drug delivery system has the possibility of further application.

We next studied the tri-modal CT/PDT/PTT efficacy and cytotoxicity of the prepared SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt in vitro. Live and dead cells can be differentiated by employing calcein AM and PI staining methods. In Fig. 6 A, a-b, no cell death is found because all cells display green PL. This phenomenon illustrates the inability of the heat generated from pure water under irradiation to cause cancer cell death and further confirms the low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs. However, a number of the cells were destroyed once incubated with SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt without laser irradiation (Fig. 6A, c). Similarly, upon 808 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2, 5 min), parts of the cells are destroyed after incubation with 50 µg/mL SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs-Apt (Fig. 6A, d) because the ease with which 1O2 is produced and the inability to generate heat under such low laser power doses, as previously discussed [41]. Thus, PDT is predominant at low laser power doses. When incubated with SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt under irradiation by 808 nm laser (0.5 W/cm2, 5 min), most cells were killed (Fig. 6A, e), as indicated by the intense homogeneous red FL. By contrast, upon 2.0 W/cm of laser irradiation, essentially all cells are killed, as indicated by the intense homogeneous red fluorescence obtained (Fig. 6A, f). This finding is attributed to the fact that at high power doses, SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs can simultaneously generate OH
and
O2 −, release DOX and produce heat to efficiently kill cells by simultaneous PDT, CT, and PTT using a single NIR laser.

To evaluate the trimodal CT/PDT/PTT efficacy of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt, we performed MTT assay. In SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt-only (0–60 µg/mL) groups, the viabilities of both HBE cells and HeLa cells decreased significantly as the concentration of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt increased, and approximately 35% cancer cell mortality rate was achieved at a concentration of 60 µg/mL. In the PDT group (0.5 W/cm2), cell viability decreases with increasing concentration of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs-Apt; 30% of the cells remain alive even at a high concentration of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs-Apt (60 µg/mL). As shown in Fig. 6B, C, the HBE cells and HeLa cells were efficiently killed by SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt under irradiation (0.5 W/cm2), even under concentration as low as 60 μg/mL. However, in the CT/PDT/PTT groups (2 W/cm2), cell viability drastically decreases as the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt concentration increases from 10 to 60 µg/mL. A mortality rate of ≈95% is obtained, which indicates that the simultaneous effects of PDT, CT, and PTT can efficiently kill cancer cells. This result agrees with findings from calcein AM and PI staining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo MR imaging of tumor
ce:section.ce:para: T2 modal MRI of tumor-bearing mice was also recorded at various time intervals: before injection, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 h after injection, as shown in Fig. 6D. Great contrast enhancement (darken on the T2 weighted MR images) could be observed in the mouse body after post-injection, that, in contrast to pre-injection, demonstrating that as-synthesized can simultaneously enhance T2 relaxation in the circulating system. It is now concluded from these data that SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs can also be used for long-term blood pool T2 modal MRI contrast agent, which in terms of clinical MR imaging counts for a great deal. The long-term effect is also critical to obtain high-resolution and steady-state images. The positive-contrast enhancement of nanoparticles was studied anatomically by transversal MR imaging in vivo. The pre-contrast and post-contrast color-mapped images for the tumor of the experimental mouse were recorded at the beginning. T2-weighted MR image intensity gradually decreased with increasing injection time of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs. Red circle of Fig. 6D showed images of the T2 distribution of the tumor, in which the region of the tumor becomes blue with time. What our work verify that it is the specificity of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs, which accounts for T2 enhanced MR imaging in the tumor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo photothermal imaging and CT/PDT/PTT
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the in vivo photothermal conversion effect generated by the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt, we used an IR thermal mapping apparatus was employed as a vivid record of the change in temperature in the tumor area (Fig. 7 A). The IR thermographic images of mice showed that the temperature at the tumor site could rapidly reach to 47 °C in the presence of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt upon NIR laser illumination (within 3 min). And after prolonged treatment, the local tumor temperature reached over 52 °C (within 5 min). This temperature is sufficient to cause irreversible damage to cancer cells (Fig. 7B). In the control groups, the local tumor temperature reached secured temperature for tumor, which compares with the results of CT/PDT/PTT group resolution measurements. Temperatures exceeding 50 °C would cause irreversible damage to cells and tissues.

Encouraged by the above results, we then proceeded to estimate the synergistic antitumor efficiency of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt on tumor-bearing mice. HeLa tumor-bearing nude mice were split into five groups (five mice per group) in our experiment. In treatment groups, mice were intravenously injected with SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt dispersed in PBS (100 μg/mL, 100 µL) via the tail vein and then irradiated with an 808 nm laser at power densities of 0.5 or 2 W/cm2 (CT/PDT/PTT group) for 5 min. The control groups included the mice administered with saline solution (PBS), mice treated with SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt (CT), mice treated with SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs (0.5 W/cm2/5 min, PDT), mice treated with SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt (0.5 W/cm2/5 min, CT/PDT). Tumor sizes were measured with a caliper every 2 d after treatment. A photograph and a polygon of mice after various treatments on the 14 days are shown in Fig. 7C, D and clearly sharp differences in the effects on inhibition of tumor volume among the five groups. The effective tumor suppression of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt in tumor treatment is mainly of the improved EPR and aptamer targeting effect as manifested by the above in vitro and vivo imaging experiments. Tumors were also collected for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining 1 day after treatment (Fig. 7E). No change is observed in the control groups (PBS). In the CT, PDT and CT/PDT group, some inflammatory cell infiltrations, cell death, and errhysis with tissue structure damage may be observed, what become the reason of all damaged cancer cells and breakdown of the nuclear membrane and shrinkage of nuclei with karyorrhexis and pyknosis were further detected. The weights of all groups of mice are relatively stable (Fig. S16A). What we have done indicate that SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt could efficiently inhibit the growth of HeLa tumor in living mice under the laser irradiation.

To validate the safety assessment, major organs, including the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys, were collected from mice 14 and 28 days post-treatment. H&E staining shows that in vivo administration of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt did not damage organs (lung, liver, kidney, spleen or heart) when compared to the control group (PBS). The lungs showed a clear and complete alveolar structure, a uniform distribution of the nucleus and cytoplasm was observed in liver tissues, the kidney tubules and glomeruli showed no obvious lesions, and the spleen showed normal white and red pulp distribution. Moreover, a normal cardiac morphology and intracellular free grainy were observed in the heart (Figs. 8 A and S15B). Furthermore, organ indices were also measured which provided information on the general toxicity. The organ index is defined as the ratio of the organ wet weight to the body weight (g/g). The increase of the organ index means organ congestion, swelling, proliferation, or function is strengthening while the decrease usually means that the organ is shrunken or the function is weakened. Typical measurement data are shown in Figs. 8B and S16B. There is no significant difference between the heart, lung, kidney indices of the control and experimental groups, showing that CT/PDT/PTT never cause obvious toxicity or side effects on the heart, lung, and spleen. But a significant difference was observed in the liver and kidney after 14 days of inoculation indices of the control and experimental groups (Fig. 8B). The good news implies that the CT/PDT/PTT group had only small side effects on the liver after 28 days of inoculation (Fig. S16B). In addition, no abnormal clinical symptoms or behaviors were found in either the PBS group or the experimental groups, during the entire study course. Taken together, these results validate that CT/PDT/PTT does not induce any apparent toxicity in mice.

The photomicrographs of the red blood cells (RBCs) were shown in Figs. 8C and S16C, D. Compared to free DOX-treated, the treatment of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt doesn’t obvious alter the number and shape of RBCs in the test mice. Meanwhile, biochemical analyses were carried out to quantify the potential in vivo toxicity of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs after administration. Here we report that the blood concentration of aspartate aminotransferase (AST) in SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs group significantly increases compared with that of the PBS group (Fig. 8D). However, the difference is not statistically significant. There is no differ in the portion about alanine aminotransferase (ALT) and creatinine (Cr) among two groups at 10 and 10 days post-exposure. The total bilirubin (TB) concentration in SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs group increases, but not significantly, compared with that in the PBS group. The ALT and Cr indicators are at similar levels for the mice exposed to SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs and control mice. These results have provided some basic information on the biocompatibility of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs, which provide a great promising application in cancer therapy.

In summary, we present a multifunctional nanoplatform based on Fe3O4@CQDs coated PEGylated SWCNTs, which could simultaneous serve as a drug loading carrier, an NIR photothermal heater, an NIR ROS generator, and a multimodal imaging probe, acting on imaging and combination therapy of cancer. The as-prepared SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs appears to be featured as good biocompatibility, and the conjugation of sgc8c aptamer enhances the cellular uptake capability of the material. Moreover, over the years, a number of studies about the chemotherapeutic effects have been engaged in HeLa tumor-bearing mice intravenously injected with SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt. Complete inhibition of the tumor growth in the SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX-Apt aliquots confirms they can selectively accumulate in the malignant tumor issues, which, in contrast to the control group. Thus, SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs-derived nanocarriers are a promising drug delivery system for the treatment of cervical cancer and other diseases that require specific drug targeting.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by Jiangsu six category outstanding talent (2012-NY-031), Jiangsu province science and technology support plan (BE2015367), and the Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2018.01.081.

Long-range corrected hybrid density functionals with damped atom-atom dispersion corrections
Phys. Chem.

Biomater. Sci.

Chem. Eng. J.
